(vertigo) Paroymsal Positional Vertigo: Paroxysmal Positional Vertigo (PPV) is a disorder of the inner ear characterized by sudden episodes of spinning dizziness triggered by certain head movements.
AIDS: Acquired Immunodeficiency Syndrome (AIDS) is a chronic, potentially life-threatening condition caused by the human immunodeficiency virus (HIV). It weakens the immune system, making it susceptible to infections and other diseases.
Acne: Acne is a skin condition that occurs when hair follicles become clogged with oil and dead skin cells. It often results in pimples, blackheads, or whiteheads, and can affect people of all ages.
Alcoholic hepatitis: Alcoholic hepatitis is inflammation of the liver caused by excessive alcohol consumption. It can range from mild to severe and can lead to liver damage or failure if left untreated.
Allergy: An allergy is an immune system response to a foreign substance that is typically harmless to most people. Common allergens include pollen, dust mites, pet dander, and certain foods or medications.
Arthritis: Arthritis is a common condition that causes pain and inflammation in the joints. There are many types of arthritis, including osteoarthritis and rheumatoid arthritis, each with its own causes and treatment options.
Bronchial Asthma: Bronchial asthma is a chronic respiratory condition characterized by inflammation and narrowing of the airways, leading to difficulty breathing, coughing, and wheezing. It can be triggered by various factors, including allergens, exercise, and respiratory infections.
Cervical spondylosis: Cervical spondylosis is a degenerative condition affecting the cervical spine (neck). It is caused by age-related wear and tear of the spinal discs and joints, leading to neck pain, stiffness, and sometimes numbness or weakness in the arms or hands.
Chicken pox: Chickenpox is a highly contagious viral infection caused by the varicella-zoster virus. It is characterized by an itchy rash of red spots and fluid-filled blisters, along with flu-like symptoms such as fever and fatigue.
Chronic cholestasis: Chronic cholestasis is a condition characterized by reduced or blocked bile flow from the liver, leading to the accumulation of bile acids and other toxins in the bloodstream. It can cause symptoms such as jaundice, itching, and abdominal pain.
Common Cold: The common cold is a viral infection of the upper respiratory tract that causes symptoms such as a runny or stuffy nose, sore throat, cough, and mild body aches. It is usually mild and resolves on its own within a week or two.
Dengue: Dengue is a mosquito-borne viral infection that causes flu-like symptoms such as high fever, severe headache, joint and muscle pain, nausea, and rash. In severe cases, it can lead to dengue hemorrhagic fever or dengue shock syndrome, which can be life-threatening.
Diabetes: Diabetes is a chronic condition characterized by high levels of sugar (glucose) in the blood. It occurs when the body either does not produce enough insulin or cannot effectively use the insulin it produces. Diabetes can lead to serious complications, including heart disease, stroke, kidney failure, and blindness.
Dimorphic hemmorhoids(piles): Hemorrhoids, also known as piles, are swollen and inflamed veins in the rectum and anus that cause discomfort, pain, itching, and bleeding during bowel movements. They can be internal or external and may result from straining during bowel movements, chronic constipation or diarrhea, pregnancy, or obesity.
Drug Reaction: Drug reactions, also known as adverse drug reactions (ADRs), are unintended and harmful reactions to medications. They can range from mild symptoms such as nausea and rash to severe reactions such as anaphylaxis or organ damage.
Fungal infection: Fungal infections are caused by fungi that can infect the skin, nails, hair, or mucous membranes. Common fungal infections include athlete's foot, ringworm, yeast infections, and fungal nail infections.
GERD: Gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD) is a chronic digestive disorder characterized by acid reflux and heartburn. It occurs when stomach acid flows back into the esophagus, causing irritation and inflammation.
Gastroenteritis: Gastroenteritis, often referred to as stomach flu, is inflammation of the stomach and intestines caused by viral, bacterial, or parasitic infections. It leads to symptoms such as diarrhea, nausea, vomiting, abdominal cramps, and fever.
Heart attack: A heart attack, also known as myocardial infarction, occurs when blood flow to a part of the heart is blocked, causing damage to the heart muscle. Symptoms include chest pain or discomfort, shortness of breath, nausea, and lightheadedness.
Hepatitis B: Hepatitis B is a viral infection that affects the liver and can lead to acute or chronic liver inflammation. It is transmitted through contact with infected blood or bodily fluids and can cause symptoms such as fatigue, jaundice, abdominal pain, and nausea.
Hepatitis C: Hepatitis C is a viral infection that primarily affects the liver, causing inflammation and liver damage. It is transmitted through contact with infected blood and can lead to chronic liver disease, cirrhosis, and liver cancer if left untreated.
Hepatitis D: Hepatitis D, also known as delta hepatitis, is a viral infection that only occurs in people who are already infected with hepatitis B. It can lead to more severe liver damage than hepatitis B alone and is transmitted through contact with infected blood or bodily fluids.
Hepatitis E: Hepatitis E is a viral infection that primarily affects the liver and is transmitted through contaminated water or food. It is usually a self-limiting illness, but it can cause severe liver disease, especially in pregnant women or people with weakened immune systems.
Hypertension: Hypertension, or high blood pressure, is a common condition in which the force of blood against the artery walls is consistently too high. It can lead to serious health complications, including heart disease, stroke, and kidney failure.
Hyperthyroidism: Hyperthyroidism is a condition in which the thyroid gland produces too much thyroid hormone. It can cause symptoms such as weight loss, rapid heartbeat, sweating, nervousness, and fatigue.
Hypoglycemia: Hypoglycemia is a condition characterized by abnormally low blood sugar levels. It can occur in people with diabetes who take too much insulin or other diabetes medications, as well as in people without diabetes due to fasting, excessive alcohol consumption, or certain medical conditions.
Hypothyroidism: Hypothyroidism is a condition in which the thyroid gland does not produce enough thyroid hormone. It can cause symptoms such as fatigue, weight gain, constipation, dry skin, and sensitivity to cold.
Impetigo: Impetigo is a highly contagious bacterial skin infection that causes red sores or blisters on the face, arms, or legs. It is most common in children and is spread through close contact or by touching contaminated objects or surfaces.
Hepatitis A: Hepatitis A is a viral infection that affects the liver and is transmitted through contaminated food or water or close contact with an infected person. It can cause symptoms such as fatigue, nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, fever, and jaundice.
Jaundice: Jaundice is a yellowing of the skin and eyes caused by high levels of bilirubin in the blood. It is a symptom of many underlying conditions, including liver disease, hemolytic anemia, and certain infections.
Migraine: A migraine is a severe headache that can cause throbbing pain, usually on one side of the head. It is often accompanied by nausea, vomiting, and sensitivity to light and sound. Migraines can last for hours to days and can significantly affect daily activities.
Osteoarthristis: Osteoarthritis is a degenerative joint disease characterized by the breakdown of cartilage in the joints, leading to pain, stiffness, and swelling. It is most commonly seen in weight-bearing joints such as the knees, hips, and spine.
Paralysis (brain hemorrhage): Paralysis, often caused by a brain hemorrhage, is the loss of muscle function in part of your body. It can occur on one or both sides of the body and can range from mild to severe, depending on the extent of the damage to the brain.
Peptic ulcer diseae: Peptic ulcer disease is a condition characterized by open sores, called ulcers, in the lining of the stomach, small intestine, or esophagus. It can cause symptoms such as abdominal pain, bloating, nausea, and vomiting.
Pneumonia: Pneumonia is a lung infection that can be caused by bacteria, viruses, or fungi. It leads to inflammation in the air sacs of the lungs, causing symptoms such as cough, fever, chills, and difficulty breathing.
Psoriasis: Psoriasis is a chronic autoimmune condition that causes rapid skin cell growth, resulting in thick, red, scaly patches on the skin. It can occur anywhere on the body but is most commonly found on the scalp, elbows, knees, and lower back.
Tuberculosis: Tuberculosis (TB) is a bacterial infection that primarily affects the lungs but can also affect other parts of the body. It is spread through the air when an infected person coughs or sneezes and can cause symptoms such as coughing, chest pain, fever, and weight loss.
Typhoid: Typhoid fever is a bacterial infection caused by Salmonella typhi bacteria. It is spread through contaminated food or water and causes symptoms such as high fever, headache, abdominal pain, and diarrhea or constipation.
Urinary tract infection: A urinary tract infection (UTI) is an infection in any part of the urinary system, including the kidneys, bladder, ureters, or urethra. It is usually caused by bacteria and can lead to symptoms such as frequent urination, pain or burning during urination, and cloudy or bloody urine.
Varicose veins: Varicose veins are swollen, twisted veins that usually appear in the legs and feet. They are caused by weakened valves in the veins, which allow blood to pool and enlarge the veins. Varicose veins can cause symptoms such as pain, swelling, and aching legs.
Malaria: Malaria is a life-threatening disease caused by parasites that are transmitted to people through the bites of infected mosquitoes. It causes symptoms such as fever, chills, sweats, headache, nausea, and vomiting, and can lead to severe complications if not treated promptly.
